canto_title: The Vision of the Celestial Lights and the Inscription of Justice

scenes.summary: Beatrice interrupts Dante's shared moment of joy to remind him to redirect his thoughts toward the approaching presence of the Divine.
Dante describes his overwhelming emotional reaction to seeing Love leave Beatrice, noting how his pleasure was redirected by her beauty.
Beatrice smiles at Dante and instructs him to turn and listen to what is about to be revealed.
Dante describes how the intense brilliance of the divine lightning made him realize the Spirit's desire to continue speaking.
The Spirit describes the fifth threshold of the Tree of Life and instructs Dante to watch the movements along the cross.
Dante witnesses various lights, including Joshua and Maccabeus, moving rapidly along the cross.
The visual procession continues with the appearance of Charlemagne, Roland, William, and other great leaders.
Amidst the swirling lights, the Spirit reveals the identity of the soul who is an artist among the heavenly singers.
Dante turns back to Beatrice and finds her eyes so bright and joyful that they surpass all other lights.
Dante reflects on how his own spiritual progress is mirrored by the increasing beauty of the heavenly vision.
Dante compares the profound impact of the divine light on his eyes to the way a face is cleared of shame.
The holy lights move like birds in a flock, arranging themselves into the shapes of specific letters.
The lights move to a musical rhythm, pausing briefly as they form their geometric patterns.
Dante calls upon the Muse of Parnassus to grant him the intellect to explain the complex figures he has seen.
Dante describes the letters and words formed by the lights, specifically the command to 'Love Justice'.
The lights arrange themselves into the shape of the letter 'M', appearing like gold and silver distinguished by light.
Additional lights descend and settle at the peak of the letter 'M', accompanied by singing.
A multitude of lights burst forth like sparks from burning wood, settling into the shape of a needle.
Dante meditates on the unseen force guiding these lights and observes a second pattern of beatitude following the first.
Dante prays that the gems of heaven may prove that earthly justice is an effect of divine grace.
Dante prays for the purification of the Temple from the corruption of buying and selling.
Dante laments that the heavenly host must witness the earthly theft of resources through greed rather than through war.
Dante concludes by warning the poet to remember the enduring lives of the Saints even as he writes of destruction.
